9HJC-Z1K: Born in the village of Bures, Suffolk, Elizabeth Philbrick was only a child when she crossed the Atlantic in 1630, bound for the new settlement of Hampton in the Massachusetts Bay Colony. She lived out her life in that frontier town in what became New Hampshire, appearing in the Hampton court records as late as 1674. She died there in 1677.
9S8K-KSC: Baptized in 1620 near the French coastal town of Les Sables-d'Olonne, Michelle Aucoin crossed the ocean to help build the young colony of Acadia. With her husband Michel Boudrot she raised a large family at Port-Royal, recorded in the censuses of 1671 and 1686. Her many descendants spread across greater Acadia, and she stands among the founding 'mothers of Acadian DNA.'
G7FZ-LV4: A daughter of New Orleans, Louise Jane Henrick was born in Orleans Parish in 1856 and never left the city she called home. Census records trace her through the same streets across seven decades, from childhood through widowhood. Her passing in March 1932 drew notices in the local papers across several days, a quiet measure of a life rooted entirely in one place.
GXCL-5T5: John Jackson Griffeth was born in 1658 in Isle of Wight County, Virginia, part of the early English presence along the colony's southern reaches. Like many Virginians of his generation, he eventually moved south into the Carolina backcountry, dying in Bertie County, North Carolina, in 1726. Little else of his life survives in the records, but his migration helped knit the two colonies together.
KLBP-Q54: A Virginia gentleman of New Kent County, Richard Littlepage served his community in both church and government. He was a vestryman of St. Peter's Parish and, with Henry Fox, donated the two acres of land in King William County on which the courthouse was built. Known by the rank of Colonel, he married Elizabeth Frances Austin in 1697 and died in 1717.
KVGX-5D1: Baptized in 1616 in the London parish of St Giles without Cripplegate, Susanna Cooke sailed to New England and married Richard Singletary, an early proprietor of Salisbury and Haverhill, Massachusetts. Together they raised six children whose families spread across the colonies and beyond. She lived to 1682, a matriarch of one of the founding families of the Merrimack Valley.
L24W-NM2: A carpenter by trade, Marin Janot Lachapelle left France to join the struggling settlement of Montreal, arriving in New France in November 1653. His skills were sorely needed in the young colony. His life ended tragically by drowning in the St. Lawrence River in 1664, a reminder of the dangers that shadowed every venture in the new land.
L416-BTY: Born around 1778 in the Ninety-Six District of South Carolina, Samuel Reuben Gentry Sr. settled in Williamson County, Tennessee, where he married Frances Cannon and raised a growing family. He served in the military during the War of 1812. His death around 1815 left seven young children, whose claims to his estate were settled through a court division among their relatives.
L5GZ-SFC: Francis Hester crossed from England to Virginia in 1704, settling in what was then New Kent County with a tract of 300 acres. By the time the lands of St. Paul's Parish were being walked and bounded a few years later, he was remembered as 'an Ancient man' too frail to travel far. He died around 1720 and may rest at historic St. Peter's Parish church.
L6JV-CGK: Descended from French Huguenots who had taken refuge in England before coming to Virginia, William Jeter was born in 1711 and married Margaret Vaughn around 1741. He later moved his family south to Edgefield, South Carolina. During the American Revolution he gave patriotic service by furnishing supplies, and he lived to about 1797, surrounded by a large family of sons.
LB24-516: Born in 1713 in Stafford County, Virginia, Mason Combs inherited two hundred acres along the branches of Pumans End from his father John, who died when Mason was a small child. He carried his family south into the North Carolina backcountry, settling in Surry County. He gave service in the Revolutionary War and left a will at his death in 1785.
LBNV-MD5: James Travers was born in 1645 in Newbury, Massachusetts, into the first generation of the Bay Colony's English settlers. When King Philip's War erupted, he took up arms, serving in the bloody fighting around Brookfield in 1675 and ranging as far as Onondaga in New York the following year. He survived the conflict and lived until 1717.
LCRS-BDW: Born in 1700 in Speyerdorf in the German Palatinate, Johannes Christian Lehman was one of twin brothers who left the Rhineland for a new life in America. He arrived at Philadelphia around 1730, joining the great wave of German immigrants drawn to colonial Pennsylvania. He made his home in that city until his death in 1748.
LDM1-CM1: Joshua Phipps was born in 1746 in Chester County, Pennsylvania, and answered the call of the Revolution, serving from 1776 to 1777 in a Virginia regiment under Captain Thomas Bowyer. After the war he pushed west into the Kentucky frontier, settling in Green County, where he applied for a veteran's pension in 1818. He lived to the age of 89.
LHG1-W6Z: Hugh Hext was born in 1650 in Dorset, England, and in 1687 sailed from Weymouth to seek his fortune across the Atlantic. He settled in Christ Church Parish near Charleston, in the still-young colony of South Carolina. There he lived out his days, dying in 1732 among the early planters of the Lowcountry.
LJKB-D7V: René Landry was born in 1632 in Martaizé, France, and became a farmer and fisherman in the Acadian settlement of Port-Royal. By the 1680s he had built a substantial homestead, with cattle, sheep, guns, and cleared land of his own. He died in 1693, one of the patriarchs of the Landry families who would spread throughout the Maritime provinces.
LKSG-19R: Thomas Tinsley Jr. was born in 1638 in St. Peter's Parish, New Kent County, Virginia, and built his life as a Virginia landowner. He patented acreage along the Rappahannock in 1689 and secured a 400-acre headright grant in 1704 for transporting twenty-eight settlers to the colony. He set down his will in 1715 and died the following year in Essex County.
LRXH-HQ3: Col. Thomas Parke was born in 1684 in Old Rappahannock County, Virginia, and worked as a schoolmaster, a respected calling in the colonial backcountry. He settled at Ballingers Mountain in Albemarle County, where he raised a family of sons and daughters. His will, written in 1752, was proved in 1761, leaving his estate chiefly to his eldest son Thomas.
LTL9-J96: George Meridas Parks was born in 1759 in Amherst County, Virginia, and grew up on the Yadkin River frontier of North Carolina. At just seventeen he enlisted in 1776 as a sergeant of mounted riflemen, serving through the Revolution under Captains Lenoir and Allen and Colonel Benjamin Cleveland. He later migrated to Indiana, raising fourteen children across two marriages, and died in Ellettsville in 1837.
LVYD-397: Born around 1654 in Virginia, William Lea rose to prominence as a colonel, judge, and burgess, moving in the inner circle of Virginia's leading families. He helped found King and Queen County in 1691 and was elected one of its first burgesses, and by 1699 he commanded the county militia. His life ended abruptly in 1703 when he fell from his horse.
LY1Y-YT5: John Nisbet was born in 1705 in Edinburgh, Scotland, grandson of the famed Covenanter martyr John Nisbet of Hardhill. He married Sarah Brevard and in 1731 sailed for America, with a son born at sea during the crossing. Landing in Pennsylvania, the family migrated south to Rowan County, North Carolina, where he settled at Mill Bridge and died in 1755.
LYR4-4KR: William A. Thomas was born in England in 1841 and immigrated to the United States as a child in 1849. He made his home in New Orleans, where census records track him through the city across several decades. He lived in Louisiana until his death in 1922, an English-born citizen of the bustling river port.
LZ6X-BTB: Mary Bathsheba Lovett lived her short life entirely in colonial Virginia, born around 1645 and the daughter of a church warden in South Norfolk. A will recorded in her name in 1672 at Lynnhaven Parish offers one of the few documentary glimpses of her. She died young in 1676, around the age of thirty-one.
LZGX-VLX: Born in 1620 in Salisbury, England, John Greene immigrated to New England as a youth and helped establish the early settlement of Narragansett in Rhode Island. He rose to become Captain and then 'Major of the Main,' the colony's highest military rank, was named in the 1663 royal charter, introduced privateering to Rhode Island, and served as Deputy Governor. He died in 1708 at the age of 88.
M3TF-47Z: William Johnston was born in 1649 at Caskieben in Aberdeenshire, Scotland, and made his way across the Atlantic to settle in colonial Virginia. He lived there for the rest of his life, leaving a will recorded in September 1719. Though few records of his story survive, his journey carried the Johnston name from the Scottish lowlands to the New World.
MMRW-T5K: Major Thomas Owsley was born in 1658 in Stogursey, Somerset, England, and his life was marked by adventure: captured by Algerian pirates in 1679, he was ransomed home through a collection taken up in his father's parish. By 1677 he had reached Virginia, where he became a court justice, sheriff of Stafford County, captain of the Rangers, and a member of the House of Burgesses before his death in 1700.
PWBD-65M: Capt. Thomas Harrison was born in 1665 in Stafford County, Virginia, and lived his entire life in that colonial Virginia community, dying there in 1746 at the age of eighty-one. He carried the rank of captain among the local gentry. Few personal details survive, but his long life spanned the maturing of the Virginia frontier into a settled society.
